text.title: react-component-architecture Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for react-component-architecture. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Place shared UI primitives in src/components/ui/, feature-specific components in src/features/FEATURE/components/, and shared hooks in src/hooks/. Do not put feature components in src/components/.
rule.rationale: Mixing feature-specific and shared components in one folder makes boundaries implicit and leads to cross-feature imports that are hard to detect. The split is the enforcement boundary.

rule.statement: Every component lives in its own folder containing: Component.tsx, Component.test.tsx, Component.stories.tsx, index.ts. Add Component.module.css if using CSS Modules. Do not place these files in the parent directory.
rule.rationale: Colocation makes renames mechanical (rename the folder), keeps context local, and prevents stale imports after moves.

rule.statement: Export components using named exports only. Never use default exports for components.
rule.rationale: Default exports break refactor tools (rename-symbol), create ambiguous import names, and are not tree-shaken reliably by all bundlers.

rule.statement: Cap component file size at 250 lines. If a component exceeds this, extract subcomponents or move logic into a custom hook before continuing.
rule.rationale: Files over 250 lines are doing more than one thing. The cap is a forcing function that surfaces the extraction opportunity before it becomes a refactoring project.

rule.statement: Install eslint-plugin-boundaries and configure it so that src/features/A cannot import from src/features/B internal paths. Exports from feature index.ts files are the only permitted cross-feature surface.
rule.rationale: Feature-module boundaries are nominal without enforcement. Without ESLint rules, agents silently create cross-feature imports that grow into circular dependency traps.

rule.statement: Use forwardRef on every UI primitive that may need a ref (Button, Input, Combobox triggers, Radix primitives). On React 19, drop forwardRef — refs are props on function components. Pin the React version in agent prompts.
rule.rationale: forwardRef is required for React 18 ref forwarding. Mixing React 18 forwardRef and React 19 ref-as-prop syntax in the same codebase produces runtime errors. Pin the version to prevent agents from mixing the two.

rule.statement: Use cva (class-variance-authority) for variant management only when a component has two or more variants. Single-variant cva calls are noise.
rule.rationale: cva adds a runtime dependency and boilerplate. It pays off only when multiple variants need to be composed safely. Single-variant components are better served by a simple className string.

rule.statement: Export compound subcomponents as named exports (export function CardHeader) AND assign as properties (Card.Header = CardHeader). The property assignment is ergonomic sugar; the named export is required for tree-shaking.
rule.rationale: If only Card.Header exists (no named export), bundlers cannot tree-shake the subcomponent when it is not used. Named export preserves the optimization while keeping the ergonomic namespace API.

rule.statement: Put all data fetching in feature hooks (or RSC), never inside leaf components. The presenter component receives only plain data props and renders them.
rule.rationale: Data fetching in leaf components makes testing slow (requires mocking fetch), disables RSC optimization, and couples rendering to network state. Extracting to hooks makes presenters testable with plain data.

rule.statement: When implementing polymorphic as props, include a TypeScript usage test that verifies href is typed on <Box as="a">. Agents frequently produce generics that compile but do not type the element-specific attributes.
rule.rationale: The common mistake is Box<T> that compiles but strips element-specific props. A compile-time test catches this before it reaches a PR review.
